result:
I keep thinking about the single cicada shell I found clamped to the fence this morning—papery, amber, split perfectly down the back like a display case someone forgot to close. I turned it over and the whole underside was a miniature cathedral: hollow corridors where legs once were, the ghost outline of eyes. It had outlived its owner by weeks, maybe months, but was still holding on with the same fierce grip it used to survive the molt.

There’s something unfair in how the discarded armor gets to last longer than the creature it protected—like finding a love letter folded inside a library book only to learn both sender and addressee are gone. And yet the husk keeps performing its duty: reminding anything with a pulse what happens when you finally outgrow the only shape you’ve ever known.

I pocketed it. You’re not supposed to—I know—but I needed the evidence that transformation doesn’t always look like sunrise and confetti. Sometimes it looks like ripping straight through your own sternum because the universe couldn’t be bothered to install zippers. Sometimes it looks like leaving the old skin behind still clinging to its post as if nothing unusual has happened.

I think we carry these husks without knowing. The version of me that used to stutter when ordering coffee, that believed every plane overhead might be coming down in fire—that self is still gripping some invisible fence rail in an old neighborhood. I pass it every so often in memory and nod, quick, embarrassed to be caught in the daylight with newer wings while it stays behind, translucent and loyal.

The shell weighs nothing in my hand, yet I keep reaching for it throughout the day, a tiny worry stone carved by entropy itself. Each time my thumb traces the seam where everything burst open, I’m back in that moment: the night I left the apartment at 3 a.m. because the walls had become a second skin I couldn’t breathe in, the taxi’s tail lights disappearing around the corner like two eyes finally closing. I didn’t know whether to feel heroic or insane—probably both. I still don’t. But I think the cicada would get it.

At twilight I put the husk back outside, pressed against the bark of the maple this time. The wind is doing a slow comb-through of the leaves, producing that hush we all pretend is silence but which is actually millions of tiny collisions—a sound not unlike applause if you let your ears forget the orchestra.

—I didn’t keep you as a trophy, I tell the abandoned self still clenched around nothing. I’m giving you a slightly better view.
